op.name: fill_diagonal
op.notes: Fill main diagonal of given array. Cost: min(m,n).
op.summary: Fill the main diagonal of the given array of any dimensionality.

blocks.text: Fill the main diagonal of the given array of any dimensionality.

For an array `a` with ``a.ndim >= 2``, the diagonal is the list of
values ``a[i, ..., i]`` with indices ``i`` all identical.  This function
modifies the input array in-place without returning a value.

desc_blocks.text: Array whose diagonal is to be filled in-place.

Value(s) to write on the diagonal. If `val` is scalar, the value is
written along the diagonal. If array-like, the flattened `val` is
written along the diagonal, repeating if necessary to fill all
diagonal entries.

For tall matrices in NumPy version up to 1.6.2, the
diagonal "wrapped" after N columns. You can have this behavior
with this option. This affects only tall matrices.

blocks.text:
This functionality can be obtained via `diag_indices`, but internally
this version uses a much faster implementation that never constructs the
indices and uses simple slicing.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> a = np.zeros((3, 3), int)
>>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 5)
>>> a
array([[5, 0, 0],
       [0, 5, 0],
       [0, 0, 5]])

blocks.text: The same function can operate on a 4-D array:
We only show a few blocks for clarity:
The wrap option affects only tall matrices:

blocks.code:
>>> # tall matrices no wrap
>>> a = np.zeros((5, 3), int)
>>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4)
>>> a
array([[4, 0, 0],
       [0, 4, 0],
       [0, 0, 4],
       [0, 0, 0],
       [0, 0, 0]])

>>> # tall matrices wrap
>>> a = np.zeros((5, 3), int)
>>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4, wrap=True)
>>> a
array([[4, 0, 0],
       [0, 4, 0],
       [0, 0, 4],
       [0, 0, 0],
       [4, 0, 0]])

>>> # wide matrices
>>> a = np.zeros((3, 5), int)
>>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4, wrap=True)
>>> a
array([[4, 0, 0, 0, 0],
       [0, 4, 0, 0, 0],
       [0, 0, 4, 0, 0]])

blocks.text:
The anti-diagonal can be filled by reversing the order of elements
using either `numpy.flipud` or `numpy.fliplr`.

blocks.code:
>>> a = np.zeros((3, 3), int);
>>> np.fill_diagonal(np.fliplr(a), [1,2,3])  # Horizontal flip
>>> a
array([[0, 0, 1],
       [0, 2, 0],
       [3, 0, 0]])
>>> np.fill_diagonal(np.flipud(a), [1,2,3])  # Vertical flip
>>> a
array([[0, 0, 3],
       [0, 2, 0],
       [1, 0, 0]])

blocks.text:
Note that the order in which the diagonal is filled varies depending
on the flip function.
